channel.title: Los Angeles Review of Books – Reviews
channel.description: Book reviews from the Los Angeles Review of Books.

item.title: Murky Effects
item.description: A novel of desire and domestic disorder amid a disintegrating literary establishment.

item.title: Correctional Facility: A Response to Jonathan Basile’s ‘Natural Election’
item.description: How could a UC Irvine anthropology professor and his fellow science studies scholar have such different responses to Kathryn Paige Harden’s recent book?

item.title: On Tantrum Literature
item.description: In Mark Haber’s new book, we encounter the tantrum novel in metafictional extremis—a hysterical literalization of the form’s essential nature.

item.title: Saint Alito
item.description: Mollie Hemingway offers up an uncritical biography of Justice Samuel Alito, the Supreme Court’s most conservative jurist.

item.title: A Femicide in Saudi Arabia
item.description: Haifaa Al-Mansour’s recent film explores the new liberties available to women in her home country.

item.title: California Sons: Miriam Pawel’s “The Browns of California”
item.description: Jake Wertz reviews “The Browns of California,” a four-generation biography of the Brown family and a portrait of the state they shaped.

item.title: All Humanity Now Stands on the Edge of the Abyss
item.description: Vasily Grossman’s fearless and compelling war journalism demonstrates his courage and integrity as a writer and a man.

item.title: The Estranged Worlds of J. G. Ballard
item.description: An incisive and moving new critical biography of one of the nuclear age’s greatest visionaries.

item.title: Organizing Pessimism
item.description: André Breton, necropolitics, and 100 years of surrealism.

item.title: Sinking City
item.description: Dorothy Tse’s new novel is a dystopian parable of Hong Kong subsiding out of memory.

item.title: Wrong Place, Wrong Time
item.description: A new movie about AI judges serves to expose how film noir was always about a world of artificial intelligence.

item.title: The Declaration Alone Can’t Save Us
item.description: Danielle Allen’s rereleased reading of our founding document is surprisingly, and often maddeningly, ahistorical.

item.title: At the Very Least We Should Know Your Name
item.description: Empire, language, violence, and metamorphosis in a kaleidoscopic novel of the Apache Wars.

item.title: The Expert as Tourist
item.description: Beverly Gage takes a road trip through historic sites from 1776 to today, discovering optimism for our political future along the way.
